**Ghent University** ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Universiteit Gent*, abbreviated as **UGent**) is a [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") located in [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"), in the [East Flanders](/wiki/East_Flanders "East Flanders") province of [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium").

Located in [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders"), Ghent University is the second largest Belgian university, consisting of 50,000 students and 9,000 staff members. The university also supports the [Ghent University Library](/wiki/Ghent_University_Library "Ghent University Library") (including the famous [Boekentoren](/wiki/Boekentoren "Boekentoren")) and the [Ghent University Hospital](/wiki/Ghent_University_Hospital "Ghent University Hospital"), which is one of the biggest hospitals in Belgium. In addition to satellite campuses elsewhere in Flanders and a Global Campus in [Songdo, South Korea](/wiki/Songdo_International_Business_District "Songdo International Business District"), Ghent University maintains many inter-university partnerships and programs both inside and outside of Europe.

Established before the state of Belgium itself, the university was founded by the [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_people "Dutch people") [King William I](/wiki/William_I_of_the_Netherlands "William I of the Netherlands") in 1817, when the region was incorporated into the [United Kingdom of the Netherlands](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_the_Netherlands "United Kingdom of the Netherlands") after the fall of [First French Empire](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire"). In that same year, he founded two other universities for the southern provinces as well, alongside Ghent University: [University of Liège](/wiki/University_of_Li%C3%A8ge "University of Liège") and [State University of Leuven](/wiki/State_University_of_Leuven "State University of Leuven").

After the [Belgian revolution](/wiki/Belgian_revolution "Belgian revolution") of 1830, the newly formed Belgian state began to administer Ghent University. In 1930, UGent became the first [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language")-speaking university in Belgium. Previously, [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") (and, even earlier, Latin) had been the standard academic language in what was ***Université de Gand***. In 1991, it was granted major autonomy and changed its name accordingly from *State University of Ghent* ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Rijksuniversiteit Gent*, abbreviated as *RUG*) to its current designation.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghent_University&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/De_plechtige_installatie_van_de_Universiteit_van_Gent_door_de_prins_van_Oranje_in_de_troonzaal_van_het_stadhuis_op_9_oktober_1817._Rijksmuseum_SK-A-4088.jpeg/250px-De_plechtige_installatie_van_de_Universiteit_van_Gent_door_de_prins_van_Oranje_in_de_troonzaal_van_het_stadhuis_op_9_oktober_1817._Rijksmuseum_SK-A-4088.jpeg)](/wiki/File:De_plechtige_installatie_van_de_Universiteit_van_Gent_door_de_prins_van_Oranje_in_de_troonzaal_van_het_stadhuis_op_9_oktober_1817._Rijksmuseum_SK-A-4088.jpeg)

Painting of the establishment of the State University of Ghent in 1817 when the city was under Dutch rule

### Foundation in the 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghent_University&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Foundation in the 19th century")]

Ghent was one of the largest and most important cities of Europe in the medieval period.

The university in Ghent was opened on 9 October 1817, with JC van Rotterdam as the first [rector](/wiki/Rector_(academia) "Rector (academia)"). The foundation of universities in Ghent, Liege, and Leuven that year – by the Dutch [King William I](/wiki/King_William_I_of_the_Netherlands "King William I of the Netherlands") – was part of a larger policy to stimulate academic lag across the southern provinces of the [United Kingdom of the Netherlands](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_the_Netherlands "United Kingdom of the Netherlands") (which would later become Belgium).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The original four faculties comprised Humanities (Letters), Law, Medicine, and Science, with the language of instruction being [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"). In the first year, it had 190 students and 16 professors.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Onderzoek_farmacie_Ugent.jpg/250px-Onderzoek_farmacie_Ugent.jpg)](/wiki/File:Onderzoek_farmacie_Ugent.jpg)

Pharmacy students during [practicum](/wiki/Practicum "Practicum") (1890)

In the wake of the [Belgian Revolution](/wiki/Belgian_Revolution "Belgian Revolution"), of 1830, the number of students declined, having peaked at 414. Although the faculties of humanities and science were dissolved from the university, they were restored five years later, in 1835. At this time, French also became the language of instruction, taking the place of Latin.

Ghent University played a role in the foundation of modern organic chemistry. Friedrich [August Kekulé](/wiki/August_Kekul%C3%A9 "August Kekulé") unraveled the structure of benzene at Ghent and [Adolf von Baeyer](/wiki/Adolf_von_Baeyer "Adolf von Baeyer") (Johann Friedrich Wilhelm Adolf von Baeyer), a student of [August Kekulé](/wiki/August_Kekul%C3%A9 "August Kekulé"), made contributions to organic chemistry.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1882, Sidonie Verhelst became the first female student at Ghent University, in science and pharmacology.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/2021_Boekentoren_-_University_Library_of_Ghent.jpg/250px-2021_Boekentoren_-_University_Library_of_Ghent.jpg)](/wiki/File:2021_Boekentoren_-_University_Library_of_Ghent.jpg)

2021 Boekentoren - Ghent University Library

### Developments in the 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghent_University&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Developments in the 20th century")]

In 1903, the Flemish politician [Lodewijk De Raet](/wiki/Lodewijk_De_Raet "Lodewijk De Raet") led a successful campaign to begin instruction in [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"), and the first courses were begun in 1906.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

During [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), Ghent University was initially closed due to hostilities and subsequently due to the refusal of the academic staff and the students to resume classes while [Belgium was occupied](/wiki/German_occupation_of_Belgium_during_World_War_I "German occupation of Belgium during World War I"). [Moritz von Bissing](/wiki/Moritz_von_Bissing "Moritz von Bissing"), the German Governor-General of occupied Belgium sought to make the territory easier to govern by exploiting the pre-war linguistic division. The *[Flamenpolitik](/wiki/Flamenpolitik "Flamenpolitik")* ("Policy regarding the Flemish people") was launched in 1916. The occupying German administration set up the first Dutch-speaking university in Belgium in Ghent under the name [Vlaamsche Hoogeschool](/wiki/De_Vlaamsche_Hoogeschool "De Vlaamsche Hoogeschool") (Flemish Institute of Higher Learning). Pejoratively referred to as the *Von Bissing University*, the Vlaamsche Hoogeschool was founded in 1916 but was disestablished after the war and the University of Ghent resumed its activities with French as the sole medium of instruction. In 1923, Cabinet Minister [Pierre Nolf](/wiki/Pierre_Nolf "Pierre Nolf") put forward a motion to definitively establish the university as a Dutch-speaking university, and this was realized in 1930. [August Vermeylen](/wiki/August_Vermeylen "August Vermeylen") served as the first rector of a Dutch-language university in Belgium.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Ghent_University_1910_-_Studentenvereniging_Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_Acad%C3%A9mique_d%27Histoire.jpg/250px-Ghent_University_1910_-_Studentenvereniging_Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_Acad%C3%A9mique_d%27Histoire.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ghent_University_1910_-_Studentenvereniging_Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_Acad%C3%A9mique_d%27Histoire.jpg)

[Student Association](/wiki/Student_Association "Student Association") "Société Académique d'Histoire" (1910)

In the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the German administration of the university attempted to create a German orientation, removing faculty members and installing loyal activists.

In the postwar period, Ghent University became a much larger institution, following the government policy of democratizing higher education in Flanders during the 1950s and 1960s. By 1953, there were more than 3,000 students, and by 1969 more than 11,500.

The number of faculties increased to eleven, starting with Applied Sciences in 1957. It was followed by Economics and Veterinary Medicine in 1968, Psychology and Pedagogy, as well as Bioengineering, in 1969, and Pharmaceutical Sciences.

In the 1960s, there were several [student demonstrations](/wiki/Student_activism "Student activism") at Ghent University, notably around the [Blandijn](/wiki/Blandijn "Blandijn") site, which houses the Faculty of Arts & Philosophy.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The most severe of demonstrations took place in 1969 in the wake of [May 1968](/wiki/May_1968_in_France "May 1968 in France").

### Since the end of the Cold War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghent_University&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Since the end of the Cold War")]

In 1991, the university officially changed its name from *Rijksuniversiteit Gent* (RUG) to *Universiteit Gent* (UGent), following an increased grant of autonomy by the government of the [Flemish Community](/wiki/Flemish_Community "Flemish Community"). The faculty of [Politics](/wiki/Politics "Politics") and [Social Sciences](/wiki/Social_science "Social science") is the most recent addition, in 1992.

Ghent University had a program founded by Andre Vlerick in 1953, then called *Centre for Productivity Studies and Research*. The program later evolved into a separate school called *Instituut Professor Vlerick voor Management*. Later in 1999 together with [KU Leuven](/wiki/KU_Leuven "KU Leuven"), Ghent University established Vlerick Business School merging the two MBA programs of the universities, naming the newborn institute *Vlerick Leuven Gent Management School*. In 2006, the school rebranded itself as [Vlerick Business School](/wiki/Vlerick_Business_School "Vlerick Business School"). [KU Leuven](/wiki/KU_Leuven "KU Leuven") and Ghent University are still the parent institutions of the business school where many of the school's professors teach also in Leuven or Ghent. Nevertheless, UGent still offer MBA programs even after the merger.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[*[circular reference](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Wikipedia_and_sources_that_mirror_or_use_it "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

### Organisation and structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghent_University&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Organisation and structure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/IGent_tower.jpg/250px-IGent_tower.jpg)](/wiki/File:IGent_tower.jpg)

iGent tower in [Zwijnaarde Science Park](/wiki/Zwijnaarde_science_park "Zwijnaarde science park")

Ghent University consists of eleven [faculties](/wiki/Faculty_(division) "Faculty (division)") with over 130 individual departments. In addition, the university maintains the [Zwijnaarde science park](/wiki/Zwijnaarde_science_park "Zwijnaarde science park") and [Greenbridge science park](/wiki/Greenbridge_science_park "Greenbridge science park").

### Library

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghent_University&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Library")]

Standing on the [Blandijnberg](/wiki/Blandijnberg "Blandijnberg"), the [Boekentoren](/wiki/Boekentoren "Boekentoren") houses the [Ghent University Library](/wiki/Ghent_University_Library "Ghent University Library"), which contains nearly 3 million volumes. The university library has joined the [Google Books Library Project](/wiki/Google_Books_Library_Project "Google Books Library Project"). Among other notable collections, it preserves [Papyrus 30](/wiki/Papyrus_30 "Papyrus 30"), an early manuscript of the Greek New Testament.

The university is also a partner in the development of [De Krook](/wiki/De_Krook "De Krook"), the new public library and media center in the center of Ghent that opened in 2017.

### International relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ghent_University&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: International relations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Universiteitsforum_2010PM_0603_21H7975.JPG/250px-Universiteitsforum_2010PM_0603_21H7975.JPG)](/wiki/File:Universiteitsforum_2010PM_0603_21H7975.JPG)

Ufo campus - university forum

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Boekentoren_ugent_belvedere_675.jpg/250px-Boekentoren_ugent_belvedere_675.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boekentoren_ugent_belvedere_675.jpg)

UGent Boekentoren

The university maintains many partnerships within Belgium, across Europe, and throughout the world.

Inside Belgium, Ghent University supports the [Belgian Co-ordinated Collections of Micro-organisms](/wiki/Belgian_Co-ordinated_Collections_of_Micro-organisms "Belgian Co-ordinated Collections of Micro-organisms") and the [Vlaams Instituut voor Biotechnologie](/wiki/Vlaams_Instituut_voor_Biotechnologie "Vlaams Instituut voor Biotechnologie").

Within Europe, it is a member of the [Santander Network](/w/index.php?title=Santander_Network&action=edit&redlink=1 "Santander Network (page does not exist)"), the Enlight (previously the U4) Network, and the 3i University Network. It also participates in the [Conference of European Schools for Advanced Engineering Education and Research](/wiki/Conference_of_European_Schools_for_Advanced_Engineering_Education_and_Research "Conference of European Schools for Advanced Engineering Education and Research"). In addition, the university cooperates with numerous universities for the [Erasmus](/wiki/Erasmus_Programme "Erasmus Programme") and [Erasmus Mundus](/wiki/Erasmus_Mundus "Erasmus Mundus") programs; within the framework of the latter, it heads the International Master of Science in Rural Development and the International Master of Science in Soils and Global Change (IMSOGLO).[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Beyond Europe, Ghent University conducts exchange programs on all continents except Antarctica.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Frameworks include its campus in South Korea and its 3C Partnership.
